text:
Step 1: Confirm a clean FlowPlane workspace
The recording begins with no mappings or active runtime work. Authentication and the tenant context remain available for the live workflow.

Understand the end-to-end runtime path
One raw Kafka stream will be processed independently by a Flink job and a Kafka Connect sink, with no scripted downstream inserts.

Preserve real tenant and team ownership
Existing team records remain visible because the demo resets runtime work, not the authentication or ownership model used by governance.

Verify the runtime monitor starts empty
No demo connector or Flink job is registered yet, establishing the before-state for later runtime cards and physical instances.

Verify Kafka Connect starts with zero connectors
Control Center shows no connector before FlowPlane registration, proving the connector that appears later is created during this recording.

Step 2: Register Kafka Connect through FlowPlane
The live FlowPlane form defines one logical Mongo sink runtime and its expected two-task execution model.

Keep Control Center visible before execution
The Connect screen still shows zero connectors immediately before the UI-issued command is executed against the prepared worker.

Run the exact connector command supplied by the UI
The disclosed PowerShell command applies the runtime-specific configuration while Control Center remains visible for independent confirmation.

Confirm the connector appears with two running tasks
Control Center now shows the newly created Mongo sink connector and confirms that both configured tasks are running.

Inspect the two physical Connect task executions
The connector detail view exposes task 0 and task 1, matching the two partitions of the raw Kafka topic.

Step 3: Prepare the Flink execution runtime
Runtime Monitor remains visible before submission, preserving the clean before-state for the Flink job and its physical execution instance.

Run the real Flink registration command
PowerShell visibly executes 03-register-flink-job.ps1, submitting the live Flink job with its FlowPlane wrapper and renewable short-lived authentication.

Confirm both logical runtimes are online and idle
Fresh heartbeats prove the connector and Flink job are authenticated, healthy, and ready before any mapping artifact is assigned.

Understand runtime type, logical runtime, and instance
The type describes the integration, each logical runtime represents a managed connector or job, and physical instances represent the workers or tasks doing the work.

Inspect the Flink physical execution instance
The expanded Flink runtime shows its job execution identity, online state, last-seen timestamp, and available telemetry fields.

Step 4: Create and govern mapping version 1.0.0
The mapping follows the normal review, approval, publication, and deployment lifecycle rather than bypassing governance state.

Compile and publish an immutable runtime artifact
FlowPlane produces a versioned artifact whose identifier, version, and hash can be correlated with every downstream runtime result.

Run the saved valid-record simulation
Simulation Studio previews the transformed payload through backend FlowPlane processing before the mapping is assigned to live runtimes.

Run the controlled invalid-record simulation
The same mapping rejects the missing event ID and exposes the field error that runtime policy will later route to each DLQ.

Inspect Kafka topics before live traffic
Control Center provides the before-state for the raw, transformed, Flink DLQ, and Connect DLQ topics.

Confirm transformed and DLQ topics are empty
The downstream Kafka topics contain no demo records before deployment, so all later messages can be attributed to runtime processing.

Assign version 1.0.0 to Connect and Flink
The same governed artifact is targeted to both logical runtimes so their outcomes can be compared from one source contract.

Run the version-1 raw producer in PowerShell
The visible 05-produce-and-verify-v1.ps1 command writes one valid and one invalid event only to flowplane.demo.orders.raw. No script writes downstream or Mongo data.

Wait for both runtime wrappers to acknowledge processing
FlowPlane waits for real runtime completion before checking outputs; the workflow never fabricates transformed, DLQ, or sink results.

Return to Control Center for the after-state
The topic list is revisited only after Flink and Connect finish processing the two raw source events.

Inspect the two raw version-1 source events
Control Center shows the valid and invalid inputs with their distinct Kafka partitions and offsets.

Inspect the Flink transformed output
The live Flink job creates one transformed Kafka record for the valid event, preserving run, version, and artifact provenance.

Inspect the Flink DLQ output
The invalid event creates one structured Flink DLQ envelope with the field error and exact raw Kafka source coordinate.

Inspect the Kafka Connect DLQ output
Kafka Connect independently creates one DLQ envelope for the invalid event while the sink connector remains healthy.

Inspect the Kafka Connect Mongo sink document
Mongo Express reads the document written by the real sink connector for the valid event. The demo never inserts it manually.

Open live physical-instance telemetry
Runtime cards expose the Flink instance, Connect worker, and Connect tasks with online state, heartbeats, counters, heap, and GC signals.

Diagnose and contain the invalid event
FlowPlane links each field-level failure to its runtime, artifact version, validation rule, and raw Kafka coordinate without stopping processing.

Complete the version-1 runtime proof
Version 1 passes only after each runtime produces exactly one valid downstream outcome and one invalid DLQ outcome.

Step 5: Publish mapping version 1.1.0 with three additions
v2 adds customerRiskBand, mappingSchemaVersion, and runtimeMetadataField as a second immutable artifact while preserving version-1 history.

Simulate the version-2 candidate before promotion
Valid and invalid samples exercise the candidate artifact before either runtime is allowed to activate it.

Step 6: Queue the Connect candidate replay gate
The connector is instructed to evaluate historical raw Kafka records against version 1.1.0 inside its runtime wrapper.

Process historical raw events inside Connect
Candidate replay uses the connector runtime and recorded source events; it cannot pass through a manually fabricated downstream record.

Confirm the Connect compatibility gate completes
FlowPlane accepts the candidate only after the connector reports a completed replay result for the historical inputs.

Step 7: Review the downstream schema difference
The candidate output is compared with the registered downstream topic subject before the Flink schema gate is executed.

Run the schema check on the live Flink job
The Flink runtime validates version 1.1.0 against Schema Registry and must report a passed result before deployment.

Step 8: Deploy version 1.1.0 after both gates pass
The candidate can target Connect and Flink only after replay completion and downstream schema compatibility are confirmed.

Activate the approved candidate on both runtimes
The deployment view tracks version 1.1.0 as it is assigned to the same connector and Flink job used for version 1.0.0.

Run the version-2 raw producer in PowerShell
The visible 08-produce-and-verify-v2.ps1 command writes one valid and one invalid v2 event only to the raw topic, then waits for runtime-owned outputs.

Inspect the complete raw topic history
Control Center now shows four raw events: one valid and one invalid input for each immutable mapping version.

Verify the version-2 Flink transformation
The live Flink job creates exactly one valid v2 output containing customerRiskBand, mappingSchemaVersion v1.1.0, and runtimeMetadataField v2-runtime-visible.

Verify the version-2 Flink DLQ result
The invalid input creates one additional Flink DLQ envelope with mapping, artifact, error, and raw Kafka provenance.

Verify the version-2 Connect DLQ result
Kafka Connect creates its own additional DLQ envelope while the connector continues processing the valid sink record.

Compare the two governed runtime outcomes
The same valid-and-invalid input pattern reaches distinct runtime-owned outputs under two immutable artifact versions.

Verify the second Mongo sink document
Mongo Express shows the new v2 document beside the preserved v1 result. Kafka Connect inserted both valid records with their version and artifact provenance.

Confirm runtime instances remain online
The Flink execution, Connect worker, and both Connect tasks continue reporting fresh last-seen values after version-2 processing.

Compare version 1.0.0 with version 1.1.0
The version comparison ties both successful and invalid outcomes to the correct immutable artifact and source coordinates.

Confirm version 1.1.0 is active
The mapping view shows the latest artifact deployed to both runtimes while the superseded version remains preserved for history.

Confirm authentication and heartbeat continuity
Both wrappers renew short-lived credentials and maintain fresh telemetry throughout the complete recording.

Review the final runtime deployment state
Both logical targets show the governed candidate assignment after processing, replay, and schema verification complete.

Generate the evidence-bound verdict
The report is generated only after topic counts, Mongo documents, DLQ envelopes, runtime gates, and producer boundaries are verified.

Review the operational audit trail
Creation, approval, publication, deployment, replay, schema validation, and runtime targets remain attributable to actors and timestamps.

Step 9: Inspect the final mapping lifecycle
The lifecycle view connects one mapping, two immutable artifacts, one connector, and one Flink job to the recorded decisions.

Close with the runtime-only evidence verdict
The final PASS confirms scripts wrote raw input only and every transformed, DLQ, and Mongo result came from real runtime processing.
